does: This paper presents CaC, a coarse-to-fine video anomaly detection reward model built on Vision-Language Models. The method operates hierarchically: it first performs global temporal scanning to identify anomalous time windows, then executes fine-grained spatial localization within those windows, and finally applies structured spatiotemporal Chain-of-Thought reasoning for robust predictions. To train this model, the authors create the first large-scale synthetic video anomaly dataset with comprehensive annotations including per-frame bounding boxes, temporal anomaly windows, and fine-grained attribution labels. Training follows a three-stage progressive paradigm beginning with supervised fine-tuning on spatial and temporal anchoring tasks, followed by reinforcement learning optimization using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with novel Temporal and Spatial IoU rewards that guide intermediate localization steps rather than relying solely on final accuracy metrics.
connects: This work extends recent advances in video understanding and anomaly detection by leveraging Vision-Language Models as foundation models, building on the success of such models in multimodal reasoning tasks. It responds to limitations in prior anomaly detection approaches that typically lack fine-grained spatiotemporal reasoning or rely on weak supervision. The hierarchical coarse-to-fine architecture draws from established computer vision principles of progressive refinement seen in object detection and segmentation. The application of reinforcement learning via GRPO extends recent work using RL to optimize vision models beyond standard supervised learning, while the introduction of structured Chain-of-Thought reasoning connects to broader trends in using intermediate reasoning steps to improve model interpretability and performance in complex tasks.
enables: This work enables several future directions in video understanding and anomaly detection. The large-scale annotated dataset with fine-grained labels provides a foundation for training more sophisticated video anomaly models. The hierarchical reasoning framework could extend to other video understanding tasks requiring precise spatiotemporal localization, such as action localization, event detection, or video grounding. The Temporal and Spatial IoU rewards could inspire new reward design strategies for other localization problems beyond anomalies. The progressive training paradigm demonstrates how to effectively scale Vision-Language Models for tasks requiring precise intermediate outputs, potentially applicable to real-time anomaly monitoring systems, safety-critical video analysis, or embodied AI systems requiring fine-grained scene understanding and explanation.
